problem_summary: 2023 MCM/ICM Problem E (sustainability / light pollution). The team had to: (1) build a model to assess the light-pollution risk level of any location given population, biodiversity, geography, climate and development; (2) apply it to four location types (protected land, rural, suburban, urban communities); (3) propose intervention strategies and quantify their effect plus societal side-effects; (4) pick two sites and find the optimal short/medium/long-term strategy for each; (5) produce an outreach flyer. Team 2312411 grounded the whole problem in a real geography — Shaanxi Province, China — and answered it with a weighted multi-criteria evaluation index (EWM-TOPSIS) fed by GIS-extracted raster data, then layered time-series forecasting and an optimization loop on top to test treatment strategies out to 50 years.
models_used: Hierarchical 'interactive' evaluation index system: 4 criterion factors (lighting/social/environmental/architectural) -> 7 leaf indicators (night light intensity, GDP, population, species count, altitude, precipitation, building density), mapped onto 3 light-pollution forms (light trespass A1, over-illumination A2, light clutter A3)
Entropy Weight Method (EWM) for objective indicator weighting, applied layer-by-layer (bottom-up) to convert relative weights into absolute weights from scheme layer to target layer
TOPSIS (relative proximity D_i to positive/negative ideal points) to produce a 0-1 light-pollution risk score, with quintile-based risk bands (Low/Med-low/Med/Med-high/High)
Vector-normalization dimensionless preprocessing + min/max indicator forwarding (converting minimal-type indicators to maximal-type via M - x)

ArcGIS geospatial workflow: WGS1984 projection, land-type segmentation into 4 classes, geometric centroid (shape-center) computation per parcel, Extract-Values-to-Points for raster sampling at 34 centroids
Ordinary Kriging spatial interpolation (Z*(x0)=sum lambda_i Z(x_i)) to densify sparse observation data into full-province surfaces and produce a risk-distribution map
Logistic growth model for population projection (50-year horizon)
ARIMA time-series for projecting light intensity, GDP, biodiversity, precipitation, building density; data first extended with Gaussian-perturbation augmentation before fitting

Treatment Optimization Model: three strategies (resident awareness, market standards/'environmental red line', social regulatory mechanism) each quantified as closed-form modifiers on specific indices — e.g. E11' = [1-alpha(t)]E11 for light source switching, E41'=E41(1-phi1)(1-phi2) for green building, with hysteresis/lag functions alpha(t)=alpha0/(1+t/alpha0) for adoption saturation
Time-windowed strategy comparison: mean treatment effect ΔD_i over short (0-10y), medium (11-35y), long (36-50y) horizons to pick the optimal strategy per window
Side-effect coupling models: crime rate C_i=C0(1+ΔD_i) and traffic accident rate T_i=T0(1-ΔD_i)
Sensitivity/stability test: perturbation term beta (+/-5% and random) plus Gaussian noise on night-light intensity to check robustness
key_techniques: Combining a subjective conceptual hierarchy with an objective weighting engine: EWM removes analyst bias from weights while TOPSIS gives an interpretable 0-1 distance-to-ideal score — a robust default for any 'rank/score locations' sustainability prompt

The 'interactive' index twist: instead of a plain tree, indicators feed into the three physical light-pollution mechanisms (trespass/over-illumination/clutter), so weights differ per mechanism (e.g. population dominates 'light clutter', night-light dominates 'trespass') — this gives mechanism-level interpretability rather than one opaque number
Operationalizing an abstract prompt into a real province: picking Shaanxi specifically because it contains all four required land types in one study area, eliminating cross-region confounds

GIS-to-model pipeline that turns an evaluation framework into a defensible data product: centroid sampling + Kriging interpolation + a full-province choropleth risk map (the deliverable judges can see at a glance)

Quantifying soft policy as hard index modifiers: each qualitative strategy is reduced to a multiplicative correction on a named indicator with literature-sourced effect sizes (vermiculite material 3.8-6.7%, sunshade 4.5%), so 'governance' becomes computable
Hysteresis/lag functions to model realistic adoption saturation instead of linear change — shows awareness that markets don't switch instantly
Explicit short/medium/long-term decomposition so the recommendation is a time-phased plan (Strategy 1 then 2 then 3), directly answering Problem 4's temporal ask
Modeling second-order societal consequences (crime up, accidents down) — addresses the 'potential impact on society' sub-question that weaker teams ignore
why_it_won: From a judge's view this paper wins on completeness, concreteness, and the visible data product. Every sub-question is answered in order with a named model, and the chain is coherent: one evaluation engine (EWM-TOPSIS) is reused throughout — for scoring (P1/P2), for measuring treatment effect by re-scoring optimized indices (P3), and for the 50-year time-phased recommendation (P4). That reuse signals a disciplined modeling architecture rather than a grab-bag. The real-geography grounding (Shaanxi) plus the ArcGIS raster extraction, Kriging interpolation, and the province-wide risk map give judges concrete, falsifiable, visually compelling output — the urban=0.407 vs protected=0.0046 contrast is a clean, memorable headline result. The team also went beyond ranking to operationalize each governance strategy as a closed-form index modifier with literature-cited effect sizes, then layered Logistic + ARIMA forecasting to make recommendations time-dependent, and even quantified societal side-effects (crime +2.14%, accidents -2.135%). Finally it closed the loop with a sensitivity test and an honest strengths/weaknesses section plus the required flyer. It reads as a full, self-aware engineering analysis, which is exactly the O-award profile.
section_highlights: The Summary Sheet is exemplary: it walks problem-by-problem (P1-P5) and states each headline number (urban risk 0.407; protected/rural/suburban as 1.1%/6.8%/68.6% of urban; crime +2.140%, accidents -2.135%) so a judge gets the whole story before page 1. The 'Our Approach' section restates the method for every problem as a labeled pipeline (measurement track vs optimization track). Model I is laid out cleanly: index-system figure (Fig 5), preprocessing equations, then the 5-step EWM-TOPSIS construction with an algorithm flowchart (Fig 6) for the layered weight propagation. The weight heatmaps (Fig 10 a/b/c) make the otherwise-opaque interactive weighting transparent and let the team narrate why population and night-light dominate. The province risk map (Fig 11) is the strongest single visual. Problem 4 is well-structured: two extreme sites chosen deliberately (Point 28 highest, Point 2 lowest) to bracket the strategy space, with before/after optimization line charts per strategy per site and a consolidated effect table (Table 11) driving the time-phased recommendation. The strengths/weaknesses section is specific and paired each weakness with a concrete improvement.
pitfalls_or_limitations: Many key effect sizes and parameters are asserted, not derived or fitted: alpha0=65%, the 3% above red-line recovery rule, phi material effects, beta perturbation range — these drive the optimization results but their provenance/calibration is thin

The 'interactive' weight tables (Fig 10) contain values that don't all cleanly normalize and the OCR'd matrices show inconsistencies; the layered relative-to-absolute weight propagation is described in prose more than rigorously defined
TOPSIS relative-proximity formula given (Eq 10) uses a projection/inner-product form rather than the standard D-/(D+ + D-) distance ratio, and is under-explained — a judge could question correctness

Crime and accident coupling C_i=C0(1+ΔD_i), T_i=T0(1-ΔD_i) is a convenient linear assumption with no empirical basis; the direction (less light -> more crime) is plausible but the magnitude is unvalidated
Self-acknowledged: potential-impact analysis isn't tied to specific measures, and strategies are evaluated independently without modeling their interaction/combination over time
ARIMA on indicators that were themselves Kriging-interpolated then Gaussian-augmented stacks assumptions; forecasting 50 years from short historical series is fragile and barely caveated
Biodiversity and building density use coarse literature ranges per land-type (tables 5/6) rather than site-measured data, so within-class variation is washed out
Sensitivity test is narrow — only night-light intensity under Strategy 3 at two points; weighting scheme and other indicators are not stress-tested
reusable_patterns: EWM-TOPSIS as a go-to scoring engine for any 'rank/rate locations or options on sustainability' prompt: EWM for bias-free objective weights, TOPSIS for an interpretable 0-1 score with quintile risk bands

Reuse ONE evaluation model across all sub-questions: score baseline (P1/P2), then re-score with modified inputs to measure intervention effect (P3), then feed forecasted inputs to get time-dependent recommendations (P4). This architectural reuse is itself a winning signal
Ground an abstract prompt in a real, deliberately chosen geography that contains all required categories at once (here Shaanxi for all 4 land types) to avoid confounds and enable real data

GIS pipeline template: project to WGS1984 -> segment land classes -> compute parcel centroids -> Extract-Values-to-Points for rasters -> Kriging-interpolate to a full-surface map. Produces a headline visual deliverable judges love

Turn qualitative policy into computable index modifiers: express each soft strategy as a multiplicative correction on a named indicator (E' = (1-effect)*E) with literature-sourced effect sizes and a hysteresis/saturation function for realism
Time-window decomposition (short/medium/long) + a per-window effect table to convert a single recommendation into a phased plan — directly satisfies temporal sub-questions
Always model second-order societal side-effects of your intervention (here crime/accidents) to answer 'impact on society' asks competitors skip
Pick two EXTREME cases (best/worst scoring) to bracket behavior, so the conclusion generalizes to all in-between cases
Close with a paired strengths/weaknesses-with-improvements section and a sensitivity/perturbation test — cheap to write, strong O-award signal
